serving.natural_language_portion: A typical tandoori platter serving is approximately 400 g, representing an assortment of tandoori-grilled chicken pieces suitable for one to two persons as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Tandoori Chicken (per 100 g edible portion)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports tandoori chicken as a discrete entry. A tandoori platter is an assortment of tandoori chicken preparations; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken entry as the primary protein component. The platter may include minor accompaniments (mint chutney, onion salad, lemon wedge) that marginally affect overall macros; the per-100 g values here represent the tandoori chicken component, which constitutes the dominant nutritional mass of the platter.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Chicken, tandoori / high-protein low-carbohydrate meat dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite tandoori platter. Tandoori chicken is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate dish (approximately 2.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g). GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous high-protein, spiced grilled chicken preparations reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with the principle that protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate meat dishes have low GI values (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (30 × 8.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.5, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt
hypertension.guidance: Tandoori platter contains approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from the spiced yogurt marinade and added salt; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation to keep a single serving within a 600–800 mg sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, tandoori platter produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion and is a suitable high-protein main course for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided accompaniments such as naan or rice are accounted for separately.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate saturated fat and beneficial spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori chicken is a lean, grilled protein source with approximately 1.8 g saturated fat per 100 g; the anti-inflammatory spice marinade (turmeric, cumin, ginger, garlic) may confer additional cardiovascular benefit, making this a heart-friendly choice when consumed without high-fat accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Platter is an Indian cuisine assortment featuring various chicken preparations cooked in a traditional tandoor clay oven at very high temperatures (typically 400–480°C). The chicken is marinated in a mixture of yogurt, tandoori masala, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, red chilli, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, then skewered and roasted in the tandoor until charred on the outside and juicy within. The result is a smoky, deeply spiced, slightly charred exterior with tender, moist meat inside. A typical platter may include tandoori chicken legs, thighs, or breast pieces, served with mint-coriander chutney, sliced onion rings, lemon wedges, and occasionally a small portion of naan or roti. Nutritionally, tandoori chicken is a high-protein (approximately 22 g per 100 g), low-carbohydrate (approximately 2 g per 100 g), moderate-fat dish with an estimated glycemic index of 30, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly Indian restaurant options. The sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally gluten-free and typically halal. In Singapore, tandoori platters are popular at Indian restaurants along Serangoon Road (Little India) and in food courts, often ordered as a shared starter or main course. Clinically, this dish is recommended for high-protein dietary needs, weight management, and low-glycaemic meal planning.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tandoori platter?
Is tandoori chicken good for diabetics?
What is in a tandoori platter?
Is tandoori chicken high in protein?
Best Indian grilled chicken dish for weight loss

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken entry, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. The platter format introduces minor variability from accompaniments, and GI is an analogous estimate rather than a direct measurement, justifying medium rather than high confidence.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database for tandoori chicken. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for tandoori chicken. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in grilled chicken preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data. GI is an analogous estimate of 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled chicken dishes; no direct GI measurement for tandoori platter exists in the approved database. The platter is served at Anjana Kitchen, Six Battery Road, Singapore, at SGD 51.00. Nutrient values represent the tandoori chicken component per 100 g edible portion; accompaniments (chutney, onion, lemon) contribute negligible macronutrients at typical serving proportions. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean Indian cuisine context: Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all mandatory per G6 rules for Indian cuisine served in Singapore.
